description: A complete blood count (CBC) is a blood test used to evaluate your overall health and detect a wide range of disorders, including anemia, infection and leukemia.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is a common hematology test used to measure inflammation in the body.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: Blood group testing to determine ABO type and Rh factor for transfusion compatibility and clinical use.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: Fasting blood sugar test measures glucose levels after fasting for at least 8 hours. Used to diagnose diabetes and prediabetes.
preparationInstructions: Fast for at least 8 hours before the test. Only water is allowed.

description: Post prandial blood sugar test measures glucose levels 2 hours after eating. Used to diagnose diabetes and monitor treatment.
preparationInstructions: Eat a normal meal 2 hours before the test. Do not eat or drink anything else during this time.

description: Hemoglobin A1c test measures average blood sugar levels over the past 2-3 months. Used to diagnose and monitor diabetes.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: Lipid profile test measures cholesterol and triglycerides in the blood. Used to assess cardiovascular risk.
preparationInstructions: Fast for 9-12 hours before the test. Only water is allowed.

description: Liver function test measures enzymes, proteins, and substances produced or processed by the liver. Used to assess liver health.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required, but fasting may be preferred

description: Kidney function test measures various parameters to assess how well the kidneys are working.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: TSH test measures thyroid stimulating hormone levels. Used to diagnose thyroid disorders.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required. Best done in the morning.

description: Complete urine analysis includes physical, chemical, and microscopic examination of urine. Used to detect various conditions.
preparationInstructions: Collect mid-stream urine in a sterile container. First morning sample is preferred.

description: Blood culture test detects bacteria or fungi in the blood. Used to diagnose bloodstream infections.
preparationInstructions: Collect before starting antibiotics if possible. Use strict aseptic technique.

description: Urine culture test detects and identifies bacteria in urine. Used to diagnose urinary tract infections.
preparationInstructions: Collect mid-stream urine in a sterile container. First morning sample is preferred.

description: HIV antibody test detects antibodies to HIV virus. Used to diagnose HIV infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: HBsAg test detects hepatitis B virus surface antigen. Used to diagnose acute or chronic hepatitis B infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: Anti-HCV test detects antibodies to hepatitis C virus. Used to screen for hepatitis C infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: VDRL test is a screening test for syphilis. Positive results require confirmation with specific tests.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required

description: Testosterone test measures the level of testosterone hormone in the blood. Used to evaluate male reproductive health.
preparationInstructions: Best done in the morning (7-10 AM). No special preparation required.

description: Prolactin test measures the level of prolactin hormone. Used to evaluate pituitary function and reproductive health.
preparationInstructions: Avoid stress and physical activity before the test. Best done in the morning.

description: Stool routine and microscopy test examines stool for parasites, ova, cysts, and other abnormalities.
preparationInstructions: Collect fresh stool sample in a clean, dry container. Avoid contamination with urine or water.

description: Real-time PCR test detects and quantifies specific DNA or RNA sequences. Used for various diagnostic purposes.
preparationInstructions: Follow specific instructions for the test being performed

description:
Reticulocyte count measures the number of immature red blood cells (reticulocytes) in the blood and reflects bone marrow production of RBCs. It is used to evaluate anemia and to monitor response to treatment or recovery after blood loss.

Interpretations:
High: Increased RBC production, often after blood loss or hemolysis.
Low: Decreased RBC production, which may be seen with bone marrow failure or nutrient deficiencies. Correlate with other blood tests.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Sickle cell testing detects hemoglobin S (Hb S) or related variants to screen for or help diagnose sickle cell trait or disease.

Interpretations:
Positive/Detected: Hb S is present; follow-up testing (e.g., hemoglobin electrophoresis) is needed to distinguish trait vs disease.
Negative/Not detected: Hb S not detected in the sample.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Direct Coombs (Direct Antiglobulin Test, DAT) detects antibodies or complement attached to red blood cells. It helps evaluate hemolytic anemia, transfusion reactions, and hemolytic disease of the newborn.

Interpretations:
Positive: Antibodies/complement on RBCs; immune-mediated hemolysis is possible.
Negative: Immune-mediated hemolysis is less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Indirect Coombs (Indirect Antiglobulin Test) detects antibodies in serum that can bind to RBCs. It is used for pre‑transfusion testing and prenatal screening.

Interpretations:
Positive: RBC antibodies present; antibody identification and compatible blood are required.
Negative: No detectable RBC antibodies.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Crossmatching checks compatibility between donor red blood cells and recipient serum to reduce transfusion reactions. It is performed after antibody screening or when clinically significant antibodies are present.

Interpretations:
Compatible: No reaction; unit is acceptable for transfusion.
Incompatible: Reaction present; do not transfuse that unit.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Hemoglobin electrophoresis separates hemoglobin types and helps detect abnormal hemoglobins. It is used to evaluate anemia, sickle cell disease/trait, and other hemoglobin disorders.

Interpretations:
Abnormal hemoglobin fractions suggest a hemoglobinopathy; interpret with clinical context and confirmatory testing.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Serum protein electrophoresis separates proteins into fractions (albumin and globulins). It helps evaluate conditions that alter protein levels, including plasma cell disorders, liver/kidney disease, inflammation, and autoimmune disease.

Interpretations:
Abnormal patterns (e.g., monoclonal spike or marked fraction changes) suggest a protein disorder and require clinical correlation.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
A peripheral blood smear (special smear) examines the size, shape, and appearance of blood cells. It helps evaluate anemia, blood and bone marrow disorders, infections, and parasitic diseases.

Interpretations:
Findings are descriptive; abnormal cell morphology guides diagnosis with other tests.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Osmotic fragility testing evaluates red blood cell resistance to hemolysis in hypotonic saline. Increased fragility is characteristic of hereditary spherocytosis (an RBC membrane disorder).

Interpretations:
Increased fragility: Supports hereditary spherocytosis.
Normal/low fragility: Suggests other causes of anemia; interpret with clinical context.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
G6PD testing measures glucose‑6‑phosphate dehydrogenase enzyme activity in RBCs. It is used to diagnose G6PD deficiency, which can cause hemolysis after infections, medications, or certain foods.

Interpretations:
Low activity: G6PD deficiency.
Normal activity: Deficiency unlikely; results can be falsely normal during acute hemolysis.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Amylase measures an enzyme from the pancreas and salivary glands. It helps evaluate suspected pancreatitis.

Interpretations:
High: Pancreatitis or other conditions (e.g., salivary disease); interpret with lipase and symptoms.
Normal/low: Acute pancreatitis less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Lipase is an enzyme mainly from the pancreas. It is more specific than amylase for pancreatitis.

Interpretations:
High: Pancreatitis or pancreatic injury.
Normal: Acute pancreatitis less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
T3 measures triiodothyronine, an active thyroid hormone. It helps evaluate thyroid function, especially suspected hyperthyroidism.

Interpretations:
High: Often seen in hyperthyroidism.
Low: May be seen in hypothyroidism or severe non-thyroid illness.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
T4 measures thyroxine, the main hormone produced by the thyroid. It is used with TSH to assess thyroid function.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests hyperthyroidism or excess thyroid hormone replacement.
Low: Suggests hypothyroidism or pituitary disease.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) is the primary screening test for thyroid disorders. It reflects pituitary control of the thyroid.

Interpretations:
High TSH: Primary hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid).
Low TSH: Hyperthyroidism or excessive thyroid hormone replacement.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Free T3 (FT3) measures the unbound, active fraction of T3. It helps assess hyperthyroidism, especially when total T3 is misleading.

Interpretations:
High: Supports hyperthyroidism (T3 toxicosis).
Low: May be seen in hypothyroidism or severe illness.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Free T4 (FT4) measures the unbound, active fraction of T4. It is interpreted with TSH for thyroid disorders.

Interpretations:
High: Hyperthyroidism or excess replacement.
Low: Hypothyroidism or central (pituitary) hypothyroidism.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) reflects average blood glucose over about 2-3 months. It is used to diagnose and monitor diabetes and prediabetes.

Interpretations:
<5.7%: Normal
5.7-6.4%: Prediabetes
>=6.5%: Diabetes (confirm with repeat testing).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Thyroglobulin (TG) is a protein made by thyroid cells. It is mainly used as a tumor marker after treatment for differentiated thyroid cancer.

Interpretations:
Undetectable after thyroid removal: Expected.
Rising or detectable: Possible residual or recurrent disease (interpret with anti-TG antibodies).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Calcitonin is produced by thyroid C-cells. It is a tumor marker for medullary thyroid cancer and is used for diagnosis and follow-up.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests medullary thyroid cancer or other neuroendocrine tumors.
Normal: Makes medullary thyroid cancer less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Anti-TPO antibodies target thyroid peroxidase and are markers of autoimmune thyroid disease.

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports Hashimoto thyroiditis or Graves disease.
Negative: Does not completely exclude autoimmune thyroid disease.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Anti-thyroglobulin (Anti-TG) antibodies are markers of autoimmune thyroid disease and can interfere with thyroglobulin tumor marker testing.

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports autoimmune thyroid disease; TG tumor marker may be unreliable.
Negative: Antibody interference is unlikely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) stimulate the thyroid and are strongly associated with Graves disease.

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports Graves disease; also used in pregnancy to assess fetal risk.
Negative: Graves disease less likely, but not excluded.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) regulates calcium and phosphorus. It is used to evaluate hypercalcemia or hypocalcemia.

Interpretations:
High PTH with high calcium: Primary hyperparathyroidism.
High PTH with low/normal calcium: Secondary hyperparathyroidism (e.g., vitamin D deficiency, kidney disease).
Low PTH: Hypoparathyroidism or non-PTH mediated hypercalcemia.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) controls ovarian and testicular function. It is used in infertility, menstrual disorders, and pituitary evaluation.

Interpretations:
High: Primary gonadal failure or menopause.
Low: Pituitary or hypothalamic dysfunction.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
LH (luteinizing hormone) regulates ovulation and testosterone production. It is used in infertility and pituitary evaluation.

Interpretations:
High: Primary gonadal failure or menopause; LH surge indicates ovulation.
Low: Pituitary or hypothalamic dysfunction.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Prolactin is a pituitary hormone involved in lactation. It is used to evaluate infertility, galactorrhea, and pituitary disorders.

Interpretations:
High: May indicate prolactinoma, medications, hypothyroidism, or pregnancy.
Low: Usually not clinically significant.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Total testosterone measures all circulating testosterone. It is used to evaluate androgen excess or deficiency.

Interpretations:
Low: Hypogonadism or pituitary disease.
High: Androgen excess (e.g., tumors, PCOS in females).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Free testosterone measures the unbound, biologically active fraction. It is useful when SHBG is abnormal.

Interpretations:
Low: Androgen deficiency.
High: Androgen excess.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Estradiol (E2) is the main estrogen in reproductive-age females. It helps evaluate ovarian function, menstrual disorders, and infertility.

Interpretations:
Low: Ovarian failure or menopause.
High: Pregnancy, estrogen-producing tumors, or ovarian stimulation.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Progesterone is produced after ovulation and during pregnancy. It is used to confirm ovulation and assess luteal function or early pregnancy support.

Interpretations:
Low mid-luteal: Suggests anovulation or luteal phase defect.
High: Indicates ovulation; high in pregnancy.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
DHEA-S is an adrenal androgen. It helps evaluate adrenal androgen excess.

Interpretations:
High: May indicate adrenal hyperplasia or adrenal tumor.
Low: May be seen with adrenal insufficiency or aging.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
AMH (anti-Mullerian hormone) reflects ovarian reserve. It is used in infertility evaluation and to estimate response to fertility treatment.

Interpretations:
Low: Reduced ovarian reserve.
High: Often seen in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Beta-hCG is a pregnancy hormone produced by the placenta. It is used to detect and monitor pregnancy and certain tumors.

Interpretations:
Positive: Pregnancy or hCG-producing tumor.
Serial rise in early pregnancy is expected; abnormal trends may suggest ectopic or failing pregnancy.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
SHBG binds sex hormones (testosterone and estradiol) and affects free hormone levels.

Interpretations:
High SHBG: Can lower free testosterone (e.g., hyperthyroidism, liver disease).
Low SHBG: Can increase free androgens (e.g., obesity, insulin resistance).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) is a potent androgen made from testosterone. Testing can help evaluate androgen-related conditions.

Interpretations:
High: Androgen excess.
Low: May be seen with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency or androgen insensitivity.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Aldosterone helps regulate sodium, potassium, and blood pressure. It is used to evaluate hypertension and electrolyte disorders.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests primary aldosteronism or secondary causes (e.g., renal artery stenosis).
Low: May indicate adrenal insufficiency or hypoaldosteronism.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Renin is an enzyme that regulates aldosterone. It is measured with aldosterone to evaluate hypertension and adrenal causes.

Interpretations:
Low renin with high aldosterone: Suggests primary aldosteronism.
High renin: Suggests secondary aldosteronism or volume depletion.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Aldosterone:renin ratio (ARR) is used to screen for primary aldosteronism in patients with hypertension and/or low potassium.

Interpretations:
High ARR: Suggests primary aldosteronism; confirm with additional testing.
Normal/low ARR: Primary aldosteronism less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Cortisol is the main stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands. It is used to evaluate Cushing syndrome and adrenal insufficiency.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests Cushing syndrome or stress response.
Low: Suggests adrenal insufficiency.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) stimulates cortisol production. It is measured to distinguish pituitary vs adrenal causes of cortisol abnormalities.

Interpretations:
High ACTH with high cortisol: Suggests ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome.
Low ACTH with high cortisol: Suggests adrenal source.
High ACTH with low cortisol: Suggests primary adrenal insufficiency.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
17-OH progesterone is used to screen for congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), especially 21-hydroxylase deficiency.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests CAH.
Normal: CAH less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Growth hormone (GH) is involved in growth and metabolism. Because GH is released in pulses, stimulation or suppression tests are often required.

Interpretations:
High or low random values are hard to interpret; use dynamic testing and IGF-1 levels.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
IGF-1 reflects average growth hormone activity and is used to evaluate growth disorders and acromegaly.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests GH excess (acromegaly).
Low: Suggests GH deficiency or malnutrition.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Fasting insulin is used with glucose to assess insulin resistance and beta-cell function.

Interpretations:
High with normal/high glucose: Suggests insulin resistance.
Low with high glucose: Suggests insulin deficiency.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
C-peptide is released with endogenous insulin. It helps distinguish insulin produced by the body from injected insulin.

Interpretations:
Low: Suggests low endogenous insulin (type 1 diabetes).
High: Suggests insulin resistance or insulinoma.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
Gastrin is a hormone that stimulates stomach acid. It is used to evaluate suspected Zollinger-Ellison syndrome or other causes of high acid production.

Interpretations:
High: May indicate gastrinoma, atrophic gastritis, or proton pump inhibitor use.
Normal: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required unless requested by the clinician.

description:
PT/INR measures how long blood takes to clot and is used to monitor warfarin and assess clotting function.

Interpretations:
High INR/PT: Blood clots more slowly (higher bleeding risk).
Low INR/PT: Blood clots more quickly (higher clot risk).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
APTT evaluates the intrinsic clotting pathway and is used to detect bleeding disorders or monitor heparin.

Interpretations:
Prolonged: Possible clotting factor deficiency, anticoagulant effect, or inhibitor.
Normal: Major intrinsic pathway defect less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Mixing study helps distinguish clotting factor deficiency from an inhibitor. Patient plasma is mixed with normal plasma and APTT/PT is rechecked.

Interpretations:
Correction: Suggests factor deficiency.
No correction: Suggests inhibitor (e.g., lupus anticoagulant).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Serum iron measures circulating iron and is used with ferritin and TIBC to assess iron status.

Interpretations:
Low: Iron deficiency or chronic disease.
High: Iron overload or increased release from cells.
preparationInstructions: Fasting may be preferred for iron studies.

description:
Ferritin reflects iron stores in the body.

Interpretations:
Low: Iron deficiency.
High: Iron overload or inflammation/liver disease.
preparationInstructions: Fasting may be preferred for iron studies.

description:
TIBC reflects the blood’s capacity to bind iron (transferrin availability).

Interpretations:
High: Iron deficiency.
Low: Inflammation or iron overload.
preparationInstructions: Fasting may be preferred for iron studies.

description:
Transferrin is the main iron‑transport protein.

Interpretations:
High: Iron deficiency.
Low: Inflammation, malnutrition, or iron overload.
preparationInstructions: Fasting may be preferred for iron studies.

description:
Gamma‑glutamyl transferase (GGT) is a liver enzyme. The test helps evaluate liver and bile duct disorders and is interpreted with other liver tests.

Interpretations:
High GGT: May indicate liver or bile duct disease, alcohol use, or medication effects.
Normal GGT: Makes significant GGT‑related liver injury less likely; interpret with other results.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Total protein measures the combined amount of albumin and globulins. It helps assess nutritional status, liver and kidney disease, and inflammatory or immune conditions.

Interpretations:
High total protein: May reflect dehydration or increased globulins.
Low total protein: May reflect malnutrition, liver disease, or protein loss.
Always interpret with albumin and A/G ratio.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Albumin is a major blood protein made by the liver. It helps maintain fluid balance and transports substances in the blood.

Interpretations:
Low albumin: May be seen with liver disease, kidney disease, inflammation, or malnutrition.
High albumin: Often due to dehydration.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Globulins are a group of proteins involved in immune function and transport. Globulin testing helps evaluate liver/kidney disease, immune disorders, and certain cancers.

Interpretations:
High globulin: May be seen with inflammation, infection, or immune disorders.
Low globulin: May be seen with malnutrition or some kidney/liver conditions.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
The albumin/globulin (A/G) ratio compares albumin to globulins. It helps interpret changes in total protein.

Interpretations:
Low A/G ratio: May reflect low albumin or high globulins (e.g., liver disease, inflammation, autoimmune disorders).
High A/G ratio: May reflect low globulins or high albumin (e.g., dehydration).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
LDH (lactate dehydrogenase) is found in many tissues. High LDH usually means some type of tissue damage and must be interpreted with other tests and symptoms.

Interpretations:
High: Tissue damage from disease, infection, or injury; not specific to one organ.
Normal/low: Significant LDH‑related tissue damage is less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Total CK (CPK) measures creatine kinase from muscle. CK rises when skeletal muscle, heart, or brain tissue is damaged.

Interpretations:
High: Muscle damage (injury, inflammation, or other causes).
Normal: Significant CK‑related muscle damage is less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CK‑MB is a creatine kinase isoenzyme more specific to heart muscle. It can rise with heart muscle injury but may also increase with other conditions.

Interpretations:
High CK‑MB: Possible heart muscle injury; interpret with symptoms and troponin.
Normal: Significant CK‑MB elevation is unlikely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Troponin I is a heart‑specific protein released when heart muscle is damaged. It is a primary test for suspected heart attack.

Interpretations:
High: Heart muscle injury (often heart attack).
Normal: No evidence of significant troponin‑detectable injury at the time of testing.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Troponin T is a heart‑specific protein released with heart muscle damage. It is used to diagnose and monitor heart injury.

Interpretations:
High: Heart muscle injury (often heart attack).
Normal: No evidence of significant troponin‑detectable injury at the time of testing.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
D‑dimer is a protein fragment produced when blood clots break down. It is used to evaluate possible blood clotting disorders (e.g., DVT or PE).

Interpretations:
Low/normal: Blood clotting disorder is unlikely.
High: Possible clotting disorder; additional tests are needed to locate a clot and confirm cause.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Myoglobin is a muscle protein released into blood when muscle is damaged. It can rise with heart or skeletal muscle injury.

Interpretations:
High: Muscle damage (e.g., heart attack, rhabdomyolysis, or muscle injury).
Normal: Significant muscle damage is less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
ACE (angiotensin‑converting enzyme) is measured to help diagnose or monitor sarcoidosis and related conditions.

Interpretations:
High: May support sarcoidosis (not specific).
Normal: Does not exclude sarcoidosis; interpret with clinical findings.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Sodium, potassium, and chloride are key electrolytes that help control fluid balance and nerve/muscle function.

Interpretations:
High/low levels suggest fluid or electrolyte imbalance, kidney disease, or medication effects; interpret together.
preparationInstructions: ABG specimens should be transported promptly when applicable.

description:
Calcium testing evaluates calcium balance and parathyroid, kidney, or bone disorders.

Interpretations:
High: Hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, or vitamin D excess.
Low: Hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, or kidney disease.
preparationInstructions: ABG specimens should be transported promptly when applicable.

description:
Magnesium is an electrolyte involved in muscle, nerve, and heart function.

Interpretations:
Low: Malabsorption, losses, or medications.
High: Often due to kidney failure or excess intake.
preparationInstructions: ABG specimens should be transported promptly when applicable.

description:
Phosphorus (phosphate) helps with bone and energy metabolism.

Interpretations:
High: Kidney disease or cell breakdown.
Low: Malnutrition, vitamin D deficiency, or hyperparathyroidism.
preparationInstructions: ABG specimens should be transported promptly when applicable.

description:
Zinc is a trace mineral important for immunity and wound healing.

Interpretations:
Low: Possible deficiency (malnutrition, malabsorption).
High: Excess supplementation or exposure.
preparationInstructions: ABG specimens should be transported promptly when applicable.

description:
Arterial blood gases (ABGs) assess oxygenation, ventilation, and acid‑base balance.

Interpretations:
Abnormal pH/PaCO2/HCO3 indicate respiratory or metabolic acidosis/alkalosis; interpret with clinical context.
preparationInstructions: ABG specimens should be transported promptly when applicable.

description:
25‑OH Vitamin D is the best test for vitamin D status.

Interpretations:
Low: Vitamin D deficiency/insufficiency.
High: Possible excess or toxicity.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Folate (vitamin B9) testing helps evaluate anemia and nutritional status.

Interpretations:
Low: Folate deficiency (megaloblastic anemia risk).
High: Usually from supplementation.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Vitamin B12 testing evaluates megaloblastic anemia and neurologic symptoms.

Interpretations:
Low: B12 deficiency.
Normal/high: Deficiency less likely (consider MMA if uncertain).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
TB-IGRA (IGRA blood test) measures immune response to TB proteins. It helps detect TB infection but does not distinguish active vs latent TB.

Interpretations:
Positive: TB infection likely; evaluate for active disease.
Negative: TB infection less likely, but does not fully exclude it.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HBsAg (hepatitis B surface antigen) indicates current hepatitis B infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Active HBV infection (acute or chronic).
Negative: No current HBV infection detected.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HBsAb (anti-HBs) indicates immunity to hepatitis B from vaccination or past infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Immune to HBV.
Negative: Not immune; consider vaccination if appropriate.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HBeAg is associated with active hepatitis B viral replication and higher infectivity.

Interpretations:
Positive: Higher viral replication and infectivity.
Negative: Lower replication (not always inactive).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HBeAb (anti-HBe) develops after HBeAg and often indicates lower HBV replication.

Interpretations:
Positive: Seroconversion; usually lower infectivity.
Negative: No seroconversion yet.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Total anti-HBc indicates past or current hepatitis B infection (not from vaccination).

Interpretations:
Positive: Previous or ongoing HBV infection.
Negative: No evidence of prior HBV infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Anti-HBc IgM indicates recent or acute hepatitis B infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Acute or recent HBV infection (or flare of chronic infection).
Negative: Acute HBV less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HCV Ab detects antibodies to hepatitis C. It indicates exposure but does not confirm active infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Prior exposure; confirm with HCV RNA test.
Negative: No evidence of exposure (early infection still possible).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HEV IgG indicates past hepatitis E infection or immunity.

Interpretations:
Positive: Past infection or immunity.
Negative: No evidence of prior HEV infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HEV IgM indicates recent or acute hepatitis E infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Recent/acute HEV infection.
Negative: Acute HEV less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HDV IgG/IgM tests for hepatitis D virus, which occurs only in people with hepatitis B.

Interpretations:
IgM positive: Recent/acute HDV infection.
IgG positive: Past or chronic HDV infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HIV Ab detects antibodies to HIV. Reactive screening must be confirmed with supplemental testing.

Interpretations:
Reactive: HIV infection possible; confirmatory testing required.
Nonreactive: No HIV antibodies detected (repeat if recent exposure).
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HAV IgG indicates past hepatitis A infection or immunity from vaccination.

Interpretations:
Positive: Immune to HAV.
Negative: Not immune.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HAV IgM indicates recent or acute hepatitis A infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Acute/recent HAV infection.
Negative: Acute HAV unlikely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Brucella IgG suggests past exposure or chronic infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Prior or chronic brucellosis; correlate with symptoms and other tests.
Negative: No evidence of past exposure.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Brucella IgM suggests recent or acute infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible recent brucellosis; confirm with clinical correlation and culture.
Negative: Acute infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
TORCH profile screens for infections that can affect pregnancy (Toxoplasma, Rubella, CMV, HSV).

Interpretations:
IgM positive: Possible recent infection; confirm with follow-up testing.
IgG positive: Past infection or immunity.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Toxoplasma IgG indicates past infection or immunity.

Interpretations:
Positive: Past exposure/immunity.
Negative: No evidence of prior infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Toxoplasma IgM indicates recent infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible recent infection; confirm with repeat or additional tests.
Negative: Recent infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Rubella IgG indicates immunity from vaccination or past infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Immune.
Negative: Not immune.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Rubella IgM indicates recent infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible recent rubella infection; confirm.
Negative: Recent infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CMV IgG indicates past CMV infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Past exposure; immunity likely.
Negative: No evidence of prior infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CMV IgM indicates recent infection or reactivation.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible recent infection; confirm with clinical context and follow-up.
Negative: Recent infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HSV IgG indicates past exposure to HSV.

Interpretations:
Positive: Past infection.
Negative: No evidence of prior HSV infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
HSV IgM may indicate recent infection but can be nonspecific.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible recent infection; interpret cautiously.
Negative: Recent infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
EBV IgG/IgM helps determine timing of Epstein-Barr virus infection.

Interpretations:
IgM positive: Recent infection.
IgG positive: Past infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Mumps IgG/IgM helps determine immunity or recent infection.

Interpretations:
IgG positive: Immunity (past infection or vaccination).
IgM positive: Recent infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Varicella IgG/IgM helps determine immunity or recent infection.

Interpretations:
IgG positive: Immunity (past infection or vaccination).
IgM positive: Recent infection.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Anti-CCP is a specific marker for rheumatoid arthritis (RA).

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports RA diagnosis and predicts more aggressive disease.
Negative: RA still possible; interpret with clinical findings.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Anti-TP (treponemal antibody) indicates exposure to syphilis. It does not distinguish past vs current infection.

Interpretations:
Positive: Current or past infection; correlate with non-treponemal tests and history.
Negative: Syphilis unlikely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Antiphospholipid IgG antibodies are associated with antiphospholipid syndrome (APS).

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible if persistent on repeat testing.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Antiphospholipid IgM antibodies are associated with APS.

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible if persistent on repeat testing.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Anticardiolipin IgG antibodies are part of APS evaluation.

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible if persistent on repeat testing.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Anticardiolipin IgM antibodies are part of APS evaluation.

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible if persistent on repeat testing.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG antibodies are used in APS diagnosis.

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible if persistent on repeat testing.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Beta-2 glycoprotein I IgM antibodies are used in APS diagnosis.

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible if persistent on repeat testing.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Lupus anticoagulant is a clotting test associated with APS and increased thrombosis risk.

Interpretations:
Positive: APS possible; repeat testing needed for persistence.
Negative: APS less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Immunoglobulin A (IgA) reflects mucosal immunity. It is used to evaluate immune disorders, liver disease, and infections.

Interpretations:
High: Inflammation, chronic infection, or liver disease.
Low: IgA deficiency or immune disorders.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Immunoglobulin M (IgM) is an early immune response antibody.

Interpretations:
High: Acute infection or inflammation.
Low: Immune deficiency or protein loss.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Immunoglobulin G (IgG) is the most abundant antibody and reflects past exposure or immune response.

Interpretations:
High: Chronic infection, inflammation, or immune disorders.
Low: Immune deficiency or protein loss.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Total IgE is associated with allergic disease and some parasitic infections.

Interpretations:
High: Allergy, asthma, eczema, or parasitic infection.
Normal: Does not exclude allergy.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Specific IgE tests show sensitization to particular allergens.

Interpretations:
Positive: Sensitization to the allergen; clinical allergy requires correlation.
Negative: Sensitization unlikely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
C-reactive protein (CRP) is a marker of inflammation. It rises with infection, inflammation, or tissue injury.

Interpretations:
High: Active inflammation or infection.
Normal: Significant inflammation less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Rheumatoid factor (RF) is used in evaluation of rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune diseases.

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports RA but is not specific.
Negative: RA still possible.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is a screening test for autoimmune disorders such as lupus.

Interpretations:
Positive: Autoimmune disease possible; interpret with clinical findings.
Negative: Autoimmune disease less likely.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
cANCA is associated with certain vasculitides (e.g., granulomatosis with polyangiitis).

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports ANCA-associated vasculitis with compatible symptoms.
Negative: Does not exclude vasculitis.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
pANCA is associated with certain vasculitides and inflammatory bowel disease.

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports diagnosis with clinical correlation.
Negative: Does not exclude vasculitis.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Anti-dsDNA is a specific antibody for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Levels often correlate with disease activity.

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports SLE diagnosis; high levels suggest active disease.
Negative: SLE still possible; interpret with other tests.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
AFP is a tumor marker mainly used to help diagnose and monitor liver and certain germ‑cell cancers.

Interpretations:
High: Possible liver/germ‑cell cancer or liver disease; not specific.
Normal: Does not rule out cancer.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CEA is a tumor marker most often used to monitor certain cancers (especially colorectal).

Interpretations:
High: May indicate cancer activity or other conditions; not for screening.
Normal: Does not rule out cancer.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Total PSA measures prostate‑specific antigen to help evaluate prostate disease.

Interpretations:
High: Could be prostate cancer, BPH, or prostatitis.
Normal: Cancer still possible; interpret with risk and symptoms.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Free PSA is interpreted with total PSA to refine prostate cancer risk.

Interpretations:
Lower % free PSA: Higher cancer risk.
Higher % free PSA: More likely benign disease.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CA‑125 is a tumor marker used mainly to monitor ovarian cancer.

Interpretations:
High: May be ovarian cancer or benign conditions (e.g., endometriosis); not for routine screening.
Normal: Does not exclude cancer.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CA 19‑9 is a tumor marker used to monitor pancreatic and biliary cancers.

Interpretations:
High: May indicate cancer or benign biliary/pancreatic disease.
Normal: Does not rule out cancer.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
CA 15‑3 is a tumor marker most often used to monitor breast cancer.

Interpretations:
High: May indicate cancer activity or other conditions.
Normal: Does not rule out cancer.
preparationInstructions: No special preparation required.

description:
Stool reducing substances test looks for unabsorbed sugars and can suggest carbohydrate malabsorption.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible carbohydrate malabsorption.
Negative: Malabsorption less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Urine reducing substances detect sugars in urine and can indicate inborn errors or glycosuria.

Interpretations:
Positive: Possible abnormal sugar excretion; correlate clinically.
Negative: Reducing sugars not detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Urine ketones indicate fat breakdown. It is used in diabetes and starvation states.

Interpretations:
Positive: Ketosis (e.g., DKA, fasting, vomiting).
Negative: No significant ketosis.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Urine bile salts/pigments help detect cholestasis or liver disease.

Interpretations:
Positive: Suggests liver or bile duct disease.
Negative: No bile pigments detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Albumin/creatinine (A/C) ratio screens for kidney damage using a spot urine sample.

Interpretations:
High: Albuminuria (early kidney disease).
Normal: No significant albuminuria.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour creatinine clearance estimates kidney filtration (GFR).

Interpretations:
Low: Reduced kidney function.
High: Hyperfiltration in some conditions.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine protein quantifies total protein loss.

Interpretations:
High: Proteinuria from kidney disease.
Normal: No significant protein loss.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine albumin measures albumin loss and helps stage kidney disease.

Interpretations:
High: Albuminuria from kidney damage.
Normal: No significant albumin loss.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine microalbumin detects early kidney injury.

Interpretations:
High: Early kidney damage.
Normal: No microalbuminuria.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine uric acid evaluates uric acid excretion and kidney stone risk.

Interpretations:
High: Hyperuricosuria (stone/gout risk).
Low: Low uric acid excretion.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine urea reflects protein metabolism and kidney excretion.

Interpretations:
High/low: May reflect diet, catabolism, or kidney function; interpret with serum tests.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine copper helps evaluate Wilson disease and copper overload.

Interpretations:
High: Suggests copper overload/Wilson disease.
Normal: Copper excess less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine oxalate assesses kidney stone risk.

Interpretations:
High: Hyperoxaluria (stone risk).
Normal: Lower stone risk.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine calcium evaluates calcium excretion and stone risk.

Interpretations:
High: Hypercalciuria (stone risk).
Low: Hypocalciuria.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine magnesium evaluates magnesium loss.

Interpretations:
High/low: May suggest altered magnesium balance; interpret with serum levels.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine sodium estimates sodium intake and renal handling.

Interpretations:
High: High sodium intake or excretion.
Low: Low intake or retention.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
24‑hour urine phosphorus evaluates phosphate excretion and balance.

Interpretations:
High/low: May reflect diet, kidney function, or endocrine disorders.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Bence Jones protein detects free light chains in urine, used in multiple myeloma evaluation.

Interpretations:
Positive: Suggests plasma cell disorder.
Negative: Light chains not detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Urine multi‑drug screen detects common drugs of abuse.

Interpretations:
Positive: Drug detected; confirmatory testing recommended.
Negative: Drug not detected above cutoff.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Urine pregnancy test detects hCG to confirm pregnancy.

Interpretations:
Positive: Pregnancy likely.
Negative: May be too early; repeat if suspected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Renal stone analysis identifies stone composition to guide prevention.

Interpretations:
Results show stone type (e.g., calcium oxalate, uric acid) for targeted prevention.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen as requested for the selected test.

description:
Histopathology (small biopsy) examines tissue under a microscope to diagnose disease.

Interpretations:
Report describes benign vs malignant changes and other findings.
preparationInstructions: Submit specimen in appropriate fixative unless otherwise instructed.

description:
Histopathology (medium biopsy) examines tissue to determine diagnosis and disease extent.

Interpretations:
Report provides definitive diagnosis when combined with clinical data.
preparationInstructions: Submit specimen in appropriate fixative unless otherwise instructed.

description:
Histopathology (large biopsy) provides detailed tissue evaluation for diagnosis and staging.

Interpretations:
Findings determine pathology type and guide treatment.
preparationInstructions: Submit specimen in appropriate fixative unless otherwise instructed.

description:
FNA cytology examines cells obtained by fine‑needle aspiration to assess masses.

Interpretations:
Report classifies cells as benign, suspicious, or malignant.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
Urine cytology looks for abnormal cells in urine, often used to evaluate urinary tract cancers.

Interpretations:
Atypical/malignant cells suggest urothelial cancer; negative does not fully exclude.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
Fecal cytology evaluates stool for abnormal cells or parasites when indicated.

Interpretations:
Findings are descriptive and correlate with clinical context.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
CSF cytology examines cerebrospinal fluid for malignant or inflammatory cells.

Interpretations:
Malignant cells indicate CNS involvement; negative does not always exclude.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
Bronchial wash cytology examines airway samples for malignancy or infection.

Interpretations:
Abnormal cells suggest malignancy; interpret with imaging and biopsy if needed.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
Fluid cytology examines body fluids (pleural, ascitic, etc.) for malignancy or infection.

Interpretations:
Malignant cells indicate cancer involvement; negative does not fully exclude.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
Pap smear screens for cervical cell abnormalities.

Interpretations:
Abnormal results require follow‑up testing or colposcopy.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen in a sterile container or prepare smear slides as required.

description:
AFB culture detects Mycobacterium species (e.g., TB) from clinical samples.

Interpretations:
Growth: Mycobacterial infection likely.
No growth: Mycobacteria not detected (may require repeat if suspicion remains).
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Tissue culture and sensitivity identifies bacteria/fungi in tissue and tests antibiotic susceptibility.

Interpretations:
Growth with sensitivity: Guides therapy.
No growth: No organisms detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Bone marrow culture and sensitivity detects infection in bone marrow.

Interpretations:
Growth: Infection present; susceptibility guides therapy.
No growth: No organisms detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
H. pylori culture identifies Helicobacter pylori and antibiotic sensitivity.

Interpretations:
Positive: H. pylori detected; sensitivity guides treatment.
Negative: H. pylori not detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Blood culture and sensitivity detects bloodstream infection and antibiotic susceptibility.

Interpretations:
Growth: Bacteremia/fungemia; treat per sensitivities.
No growth: No bloodstream infection detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Urine culture and sensitivity detects urinary tract infection and antibiotic susceptibility.

Interpretations:
Growth: UTI organism identified; treat per sensitivities.

preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Stool culture and sensitivity detects bacterial pathogens causing diarrhea.

Growth: Pathogen identified; treat as appropriate.
No growth: Bacterial infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description: Complete semen analysis including physical examination, microscopic examination, and morphology.
preparationInstructions: Maintain 2-7 days of abstinence before sample collection. Collect the entire ejaculate in a sterile container.

description:
Vaginal culture and sensitivity detects bacterial/fungal infection and guides therapy.

Interpretations:
Growth: Organism identified.
No growth: No significant bacterial growth.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Throat culture and sensitivity detects bacterial pharyngitis (e.g., strep) and guides therapy.

Interpretations:
Growth: Pathogen identified.
No growth: Bacterial infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Wound/pus swab culture and sensitivity identifies wound infection and antibiotic susceptibility.

Interpretations:
Growth: Organism identified; treat per sensitivities.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
CSF culture and sensitivity detects meningitis pathogens and guides antibiotics.

Interpretations:
Growth: CNS infection likely.
No growth: Bacterial meningitis less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Pleural fluid culture and sensitivity detects infection in pleural space.

Interpretations:
Growth: Infection (empyema) likely.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Peritoneal fluid culture and sensitivity detects intra‑abdominal infection.

Interpretations:
Growth: Infection likely.
No growth: No organisms detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Synovial fluid culture and sensitivity detects septic arthritis.

Interpretations:
Growth: Joint infection likely.
No growth: Infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Ear swab culture and sensitivity detects ear infection pathogens.

Interpretations:
Growth: Organism identified.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Eye swab culture and sensitivity detects conjunctival/ocular infection pathogens.

Interpretations:
Growth: Organism identified.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Rectal swab culture and sensitivity detects rectal infection or colonization.

Interpretations:
Growth: Organism identified.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Urethral swab culture and sensitivity detects urethral infection pathogens.

Interpretations:
Growth: Organism identified.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Kidney fluid culture and sensitivity detects renal infection.

Interpretations:
Growth: Infection likely.
No growth: No organisms detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Bronchial wash culture detects respiratory pathogens.

Interpretations:
Growth: Pathogen identified.
No growth: No bacterial growth detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Sputum culture and sensitivity detects lower respiratory infection pathogens.

Interpretations:
Growth: Pathogen identified; treat per sensitivities.
No growth: Bacterial infection less likely.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Fungal culture and sensitivity detects fungal infections and guides antifungal therapy.

Interpretations:
Growth: Fungus identified.
No growth: No fungal infection detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Water culture checks for microbial contamination.

Interpretations:
Growth: Contamination detected.
No growth: No organisms detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Leishman bodies detection looks for Leishmania parasites in tissue/smears.

Interpretations:
Positive: Leishmaniasis.
Negative: Parasites not detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
Gram stain is a rapid test to detect and classify bacteria.

Interpretations:
Positive: Bacteria seen; guides initial therapy.
Negative: No bacteria seen (culture may still be needed).
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
ZN stain (Ziehl‑Neelsen) detects acid‑fast bacilli (e.g., TB).

Interpretations:
Positive: AFB present; suggests mycobacterial infection.
Negative: AFB not seen; culture/PCR may still be needed.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen aseptically before starting antibiotics whenever possible.

description:
HBV quantitative PCR measures hepatitis B viral load to assess active infection and monitor treatment.

Interpretations:
Detected: Active viral replication.
Not detected: No measurable HBV DNA.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HCV quantitative PCR measures hepatitis C viral load for diagnosis and treatment monitoring.

Interpretations:
Detected: Active HCV infection.
Not detected: No measurable HCV RNA.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HCV genotyping identifies viral genotype to guide therapy selection.

Interpretations:
Genotype reported to tailor treatment; not a measure of disease severity.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HDV detection/quantification identifies active hepatitis D infection and viral load.

Interpretations:
Detected: Active HDV infection.
Not detected: No measurable HDV RNA.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HEV detection/quantification identifies active hepatitis E infection.

Interpretations:
Detected: Active HEV infection.
Not detected: No measurable HEV RNA.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HIV quantitative PCR (viral load) measures HIV RNA to monitor treatment and disease activity.

Interpretations:
Detected: Active replication.
Not detected: Below assay limit; treatment likely effective.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
CMV quantification measures viral load, mainly in immunocompromised patients.

Interpretations:
Detected: Active CMV replication.
Not detected: No measurable CMV DNA.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HSV‑1/2 PCR detects and quantifies herpes simplex virus DNA.

Interpretations:
Detected: HSV infection present.
Not detected: HSV DNA not found.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HPV DNA testing detects high‑risk HPV types associated with cervical cancer.

Interpretations:
Positive: High‑risk HPV detected; follow screening guidelines.
Negative: High‑risk HPV not detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
EBV quantification measures Epstein‑Barr virus load, often in immunocompromised patients.

Interpretations:
Detected: EBV DNA present.
Not detected: EBV DNA not measurable.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
GeneXpert MTB/RIF detects TB DNA and rifampin resistance.

Interpretations:
Detected: TB likely; rifampin resistance reported if present.
Not detected: TB DNA not found.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
MTB DNA detection identifies Mycobacterium tuberculosis DNA.

Interpretations:
Detected: TB infection likely.
Not detected: TB DNA not found.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
JAK2 mutation testing evaluates myeloproliferative neoplasms (e.g., polycythemia vera).

Interpretations:
Positive: Supports MPN diagnosis.
Negative: Does not exclude MPN.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
Factor V Leiden mutation testing evaluates inherited thrombophilia risk.

Interpretations:
Positive: Increased clot risk.
Negative: This mutation not detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
Chromosomal analysis (karyotype) looks for chromosomal abnormalities.

Interpretations:
Abnormal: Chromosomal disorder detected.
Normal: No major chromosomal abnormality detected.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HLA‑B27 is associated with certain inflammatory conditions (e.g., ankylosing spondylitis).

Interpretations:
Positive: Increased risk; not diagnostic alone.
Negative: Condition still possible.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HLA donor tissue typing helps match organ donors to recipients.

Interpretations:
Closer match: Lower rejection risk; used with other criteria.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
HLA recipient tissue typing evaluates compatibility for transplantation.

Interpretations:
Results guide donor selection and rejection risk.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.

description:
Celiac HLA‑DQ association testing checks genetic risk for celiac disease.

Interpretations:
Negative for risk alleles: Celiac disease unlikely.
Positive: Genetic risk present; not diagnostic.
preparationInstructions: Collect specimen according to PCR protocol and transport promptly.
